section.title: The Commander of the LORD's Army
section.sectionRationale: On the eve of Jericho, Joshua meets a mysterious armed man and asks the natural question of a general: whose side are you on? The answer redraws everything. This is not an ally who has come to take Israel's side, but the Commander of the LORD's army who has come to take command, and the ground turns holy under Joshua's feet. It is one brief but pivotal movement, the encounter that reveals who truly leads the conquest.
section.thesis: The battle for the land is not Israel's war that God will assist, but the LORD's war that Israel is summoned to join; so the true Commander appears, sword drawn, and calls Joshua not to recruit Him but to bow, worship, and take his orders on holy ground.
section.frameNote: One movement in three quick exchanges: Joshua's challenge to the armed man, verse 13; the Commander's answer and Joshua's worship, verse 14; and the command to remove his sandals on holy ground, verse 15.

units.label: The armed man before Jericho
units.note: are You for us or for our enemies

verses.text: Now when Joshua was near Jericho, he looked up and saw a man standing in front of him with a drawn sword in His hand. Joshua approached Him and asked, “Are You for us or for our enemies?”

units.label: The Commander's answer and Joshua's worship
units.note: I have come as Commander of the LORD's army

verses.text: “Neither,” He replied. “I have now come as Commander of the LORD’s {{tsava|army}}.” Then Joshua fell facedown in {{shachah|reverence}} and asked Him, “What does my Lord have to say to His servant?”

units.label: Holy ground
units.note: take off your sandals, for this place is holy

verses.text: The Commander of the LORD’s army replied, “Take off your sandals, for the place where you are standing is {{qodesh|holy}}.” And Joshua did so.

tsava.gloss: Army, host, company arrayed for war. The figure is Commander of the tsava of the LORD, the host of heaven. The title recalls the LORD of hosts, the God who leads uncountable armies; it declares that the real force behind the conquest is heavenly, and that Joshua's men are joining a war the LORD is already waging.

shachah.gloss: To bow down, prostrate oneself, worship. Joshua falls facedown before the Commander, an act of worship that the figure receives, unlike the holy angel in Revelation 22:8-9 who refuses it. That He accepts worship, and calls the ground holy, marks Him out as more than a created angel, an appearance of the LORD Himself.

qodesh.gloss: Holiness, that which is set apart because God is present. The ground is holy not by nature but because the LORD stands there, exactly as at the burning bush, Exodus 3:5. The command to remove the sandals binds this scene to Moses' call and confirms that Joshua stands in the presence of the holy God who commissions and leads His people.

basic.context: Joshua is scouting near Jericho when he suddenly sees a man standing before him with a drawn sword. Like any commander, Joshua challenges him: are you on our side or the enemy's? The answer is startling: Neither. I have come as Commander of the LORD's army. This is not a soldier choosing sides in Israel's war; this is the LORD's own Commander come to take charge of His war. Joshua immediately falls facedown and worships, asking for orders, and is told to remove his sandals because the ground is holy, the same words spoken to Moses at the burning bush. The question underneath is: whose battle is the conquest, really, and who is in command?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, and the brief scene is heavy with theological weight. The figure receives worship and calls the ground holy, which a mere creature would refuse, so the church has long understood this as a christophany, an appearance of the pre-incarnate Son, or at least of the LORD Himself. The passage does not give battle tactics; it resets Joshua's whole frame of reference, teaching that God does not join our causes so much as call us into His.
basic.originalLanguages: The figure is Commander of the tsava, the army or host, of the LORD, the heavenly force behind the conquest. Joshua falls in shachah, worship, which the Commander accepts. And the ground is qodesh, holy, because the LORD is present, echoing Moses at the burning bush.
basic.typology: The Commander who leads the LORD's heavenly army points to Christ, the captain of our salvation who leads His people to victory, and who appears in Revelation with the armies of heaven. The holy ground and the removed sandals bind Joshua's commissioning to Moses', both standing before the same holy LORD.
basic.christ: Many understand this armed Commander who receives worship to be the pre-incarnate Christ, the same LORD who appeared to Moses. Whether or not one presses that identification, the scene reveals the true Captain of the LORD's hosts, and the New Testament names Jesus the pioneer and captain of our salvation, Hebrews 2:10, and shows Him leading the armies of heaven, Revelation 19:11-14.
basic.god: God reveals Himself as the true Commander of the conquest. He is holy, turning ordinary ground holy by His presence. He is sovereign, not a partisan ally to be recruited but the Lord of hosts who summons Joshua to His side. The right posture before Him is not negotiation but worship and glad obedience.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 3 records the burning bush, where Moses is told to remove his sandals on holy ground. Revelation 19 shows Christ leading the armies of heaven. Hebrews 2 calls Jesus the captain of our salvation. And Revelation 22 shows a holy angel refusing the worship this Commander accepts.
basic.oneStory: Before Jericho falls, the LORD reveals that the conquest is His war, led by His Commander, and Joshua must worship rather than recruit Him. The scene points to Christ, the true Captain of the LORD's hosts, who leads His people in the real battle and before whom every knee will bow.

context.literary: This short scene closes chapter 5 and opens the assault on Jericho; the Commander's instructions flow directly into chapter 6. It deliberately echoes Moses' call at the burning bush, the same holy ground, the same command to remove the sandals, casting Joshua as Moses' true successor standing before the same LORD. Joshua's soldierly question and the Commander's reframing Neither give the scene its punch, overturning the assumption that God is simply on Israel's side.
context.historical: Joshua stands near Jericho as the campaign begins, likely surveying the fortified city. A drawn sword signaled a warrior ready for battle. In the ancient world, before a war, people sought to secure their god's favor for their side; the Commander's answer, Neither, breaks that assumption, declaring that the LORD has not come to serve Israel's agenda but to lead His own. Removing sandals was an act of reverence, acknowledging holy ground and the presence of the divine.
context.canonical: The scene stands in the line of theophanies where the LORD, or the angel of the LORD, appears to commission His servants, most closely Moses at the bush. The title Commander of the LORD's army connects to the frequent biblical name the LORD of hosts, and it anticipates the New Testament portrait of Christ leading the armies of heaven, Revelation 19. Together these passages show that God's redemptive purposes advance as His war, waged and won by His own hand.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal, at the threshold of the conquest, that the war belongs to the LORD and is led by His Commander, to correct the assumption that God simply takes Israel's side, and to call Joshua, and Israel, to worship and obedient submission before the holy God who leads them.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes a unique theophany at a decisive moment; it does not promise such visions to us or prescribe a ritual. What endures is its theology: God is not a resource we enlist for our causes but the holy Lord who calls us into His purposes, so that the fitting response to Him is worship and surrendered obedience, not negotiation.

debates.question: Who is the Commander of the LORD's army?

views.label: A christophany, the pre-incarnate Son
views.case: The figure receives Joshua's worship and declares the ground holy, things a created angel refuses to do, Revelation 22:8-9; He speaks with divine authority and stands where the LORD stood before Moses, so many understand this to be an appearance of the pre-incarnate Christ, the LORD Himself in visible form.

views.label: The LORD manifest, closely tied to the angel of the LORD
views.case: The scene parallels the angel of the LORD at the burning bush, where the messenger and the LORD are spoken of interchangeably, Exodus 3:2-6; on this reading the Commander is the visible manifestation of the LORD, whether or not one specifies the Son, and the point is that God Himself has come to lead the war.

debates.bearing: Both agree the figure is divine, not a mere creature: He accepts worship and hallows the ground, so Joshua rightly falls down; the conquest is unmistakably the LORD's war, and Christ is the true Captain of His hosts.

study.originalLanguages: Three words unlock the scene. The figure names Himself Commander of the tsava of the LORD, the host or army; the same word lies behind the great divine title the LORD of hosts, the God who commands the uncountable armies of heaven. So the reveal is not that Israel has gained a powerful recruit but that the heavenly host has come, and Joshua's men are being folded into a war the LORD is already waging. Joshua responds by falling facedown in shachah, worship, prostration, the posture reserved for God; and crucially the Commander receives it, unlike the holy angel who forbids John to worship him, Revelation 22:8-9. Then comes the word qodesh, holiness: the ground is holy, not in itself but because the LORD stands on it, precisely as at the burning bush where Moses was told to remove his sandals, Exodus 3:5. Army, worship, holy: the vocabulary declares that Joshua stands before the divine Commander of heaven's hosts, on ground made holy by His presence, and that the only fitting response is to fall down and obey.

typology.type: The Commander who leads the LORD's heavenly army
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the captain and pioneer of our salvation, who leads the armies of heaven, Hebrews 2:10 and Revelation 19:11-16.

typology.type: Holy ground and the removed sandals before the LORD
typology.fulfillment: The presence of God in Christ that consecrates and commissions His servants, echoing Moses and now Joshua, Exodus 3:5 and Acts 7:33.

typology.type: The drawn sword of the LORD's Commander
typology.fulfillment: Christ who comes in judgment and victory, from whose mouth proceeds a sharp sword to strike the nations, Revelation 19:15.

study.christ: The armed Commander who accepts Joshua's worship and hallows the ground has long been understood as an appearance of the pre-incarnate Christ, or at the least of the LORD Himself in visible form. Either way, the scene unveils the true Captain of God's hosts, and the New Testament gives Him a name: Jesus, the pioneer and captain of our salvation, made perfect through suffering to bring many sons to glory, Hebrews 2:10. He is the one who rides out at the head of heaven's armies, Faithful and True, King of kings and Lord of lords, Revelation 19:11-16. The lesson Joshua learns before Jericho is the lesson the whole Bible presses on us: the decisive battle is the LORD's, waged and won by His Commander, so that our part is not to enlist God for our purposes but to fall at His feet, worship Him, and follow His orders into the victory He secures.
study.god: This brief scene reveals God as the holy Lord of hosts who leads His own war. He is not a partisan ally who lines up behind Israel's plans; when Joshua asks whose side He is on, the answer is Neither, because the question is wrong. God has not come to join Israel; He has come to command, and Israel must come to His side. He is holy, so that the very dirt where He stands becomes holy ground and sandals must come off. And He is worthy of worship, receiving Joshua's prostration as His due. The passage strips away the illusion that God exists to serve our causes and re-centers everything on His holiness, His sovereignty, and His leadership, calling His servant to bow and obey.

crossReferences.note: Moses at the burning bush is told to remove his sandals on holy ground before the LORD, the scene Joshua's encounter deliberately echoes.
Christ rides at the head of the armies of heaven, the true Commander of the LORD's host revealed in glory.
Jesus is the captain and pioneer of our salvation, leading many sons to glory.
A holy angel refuses John's worship, unlike this Commander who receives it, marking Him as divine.
The thrice-holy LORD of hosts before whom His servant is undone, the same holy presence Joshua meets.

study.oneStory: Just before Jericho, the LORD reveals that the conquest is His war, led by the Commander of His heavenly host, and Joshua must worship rather than recruit Him. The ground turns holy, as it did before Moses, binding the two leaders to the same holy God. The scene lifts the reader's eyes from Israel's campaign to the true Captain of the LORD's armies, Christ, who wages and wins the decisive battle and before whom every knee will one day bow.

quiz.q: How did the Commander answer Joshua's question, are You for us or for our enemies?

options.text: Neither; He had come as Commander of the LORD's army to lead, not to take a side.
options.note: Verse 14 reframes the whole encounter: God comes to command, not to be recruited.

options.text: He said He was firmly on Israel's side against Jericho.
options.note: Israel would indeed be given the victory, but His answer was Neither; He came to lead His own war, not to join Israel's.

options.text: He refused to answer and vanished.
options.note: He answered clearly and gave Joshua instructions; He did not vanish without a word.

options.text: He said He had come to fight for Jericho.
options.note: He had not come for the enemy; He came as Commander of the LORD's army.

quiz.q: How did Joshua respond to the Commander?

options.text: He fell facedown in worship and asked for the Commander's orders.
options.note: Verse 14 shows Joshua worshiping and submitting, the fitting response to the divine Commander.

options.text: He saluted Him as a fellow officer and shook His hand.
options.note: He treated Him not as an equal but as Lord, falling facedown before Him.

options.text: He drew his own sword to test the stranger.
options.note: He did approach and challenge Him at first, but on hearing the answer he fell down in worship, he did not fight.

options.text: He ran back to camp in fear.
options.note: He did not flee; he bowed in reverence and asked for orders.

quiz.q: What command did the Commander give Joshua, and why?

options.text: To take off his sandals, because the place where he stood was holy.
options.note: Verse 15 echoes Moses at the burning bush; the ground is holy because the LORD is present.

options.text: To sharpen his weapons for the battle ahead.
options.note: Battle was coming, but the command here was to remove his sandals on holy ground, not to prepare weapons.

options.text: To build an altar on the spot at once.
options.note: No altar is commanded here; Joshua is told to remove his sandals because the ground is holy.

options.text: To gather the elders for a council of war.
options.note: The command was personal reverence before the holy God, not a council.

quiz.q: What does this encounter reveal about the conquest?

options.text: It is the LORD's war, led by His Commander, into which Israel is summoned, not merely Israel's war that God assists.
options.note: The answer Neither and the demand for worship reframe the whole campaign as God's.

options.text: It is a war Israel must win by its own strategy and strength.
options.note: Israel fights, but the scene stresses that the war and the command belong to the LORD, not to human strategy.

options.text: It is an ordinary military campaign with no divine involvement.
options.note: The opposite: the divine Commander Himself appears to lead it.

options.text: It shows that God had abandoned Israel to face Jericho alone.
options.note: God had come as Commander to lead them; He had not abandoned them.

questions: Joshua asked whose side God was on, and God reframed the question. Where might you be asking God to bless your plans rather than surrendering to His?
The Commander came to be worshiped and obeyed, not recruited. What would change in your prayers if you approached God more as Commander than as helper?
The ground became holy simply because the LORD was present. How does an awareness of God's holy presence change the way you carry yourself in ordinary places?
Joshua fell facedown and asked, what does my Lord have to say to His servant? Is that your posture before God, ready to receive orders rather than give them?
The true Captain of the LORD's hosts is Christ, who wins the decisive battle. How does knowing that the outcome rests on Him rather than on you steady you in your struggles this week?

translationNotes.note: Fell facedown in reverence renders shachah, which the BSB footnote also gives as paid homage or worshiped; the Commander receives this worship, marking Him as more than a creature.
Commander of the LORD's army translates a title tied to the frequent name the LORD of hosts, the God who leads the armies of heaven.
The command to remove the sandals on holy ground deliberately echoes Exodus 3:5, binding Joshua's encounter to Moses' call at the burning bush.

No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
